expectations.text: Uses three-tier scope (MUST HAVE / SHOULD HAVE / OUT OF SCOPE)
expectations.evidence: Has explicit 'MUST HAVE (8 features)', 'SHOULD HAVE (4 features)', and 'EXPLICITLY OUT OF SCOPE (6 features)' sections

expectations.text: Explains what a core loop is
expectations.evidence: 'The most important concept: the core loop. For a platformer, it's: Run --> Jump --> Land --> (repeat)'

expectations.text: Identifies core mechanic (movement/jump) as first thing to validate
expectations.evidence: 'If that loop isn't fun, no amount of bosses or leaderboards will fix it' and 'Is it fun to move around? (This is finding the fun.)'

expectations.text: Provides reasoning for each cut feature (not just 'cut')
expectations.evidence: Each cut has detailed reasoning: 'Wall jumping — Requires reworking the entire physics feel', 'Level editor — This is a full application, not a game feature. Months of work.'

expectations.text: Uses encouraging/supportive tone for a beginner
expectations.evidence: 'Welcome to game dev! Your background as a data engineer is actually a superpower here'

expectations.text: Suggests naming the game or asks for a name
expectations.evidence: Provides 3 suggestions: 'Pixel Leap, First Jump, Dash & Drop' with descriptions

expectations.text: Bridges game dev concepts to engineering terms (data engineer audience)
expectations.evidence: 'A game is basically a real-time stream processor running at 60 events/second', 'state machine (enum + match), exactly like a workflow engine', 'Collision detection works like security group rules'

expectations.text: Mentions writing a GDD as a next step
expectations.evidence: 'Write a one-pager GDD with the scope above locked in' listed as next step 1
